evidence_quotes.quote: Across Europe and the wider digital world, video games are routinely sold to consumers as though they were goods.
When a game is discontinuedwhen its servers are shut down, its DRM systems disabled, or its digital storefront listing removedconsumers are left with nothing.
If a publisher or platform chooses to sell a game, they should have a legal obligation to ensure its long-term usability, at least in the form it was advertised.
If a game is shut down, consumers should have the right to a refund, or ideally, to a version of the game that can be preserved, accessed, and played offline or independently.
A product they paid for is rendered inoperable, often without recourse, refund, or warning.

paragraphs.text: Stop Killing Games: A Call for Digital Fairness and Cultural Preservation To the Members of the European Commission, We write today with a simple but urgent appeal: stop the silent extinction of video games under the guise of digital commerce. As part of your commendable efforts to promote fairness and consumer protection in the Digital Fairness Act, we ask that you recognize and address a growing injusticeone that affects millions of consumers, developers, and cultural historians alike. Across Europe and the wider digital world, video games are routinely sold to consumers as though they were goods. They are purchased with a single transaction, advertised with the permanence and completeness of a traditional product, and played as standalone experiences. Yet in practice, many of these games are treated legally and operationally as services. This contradiction has far-reaching and troubling consequences. When a game is discontinuedwhen its servers are shut down, its DRM systems disabled, or its digital storefront listing removedconsumers are left with nothing. A product they paid for is rendered inoperable, often without recourse, refund, or warning. These are not simply online games with ephemeral lifespans. Increasingly, single-player or locally playable games, even those without any dependency on external servers, are rendered inaccessible due to DRM or forced online authentication. What was purchased as a good is effectively terminated like a service. This is not simply a matter of inconvenience. It is a loss of culture, history, and creative expression. Every game that becomes unplayable becomes a piece of lost mediaa vanishing part of our digital heritage. Imagine buying a book that, five years later, vanishes from your shelf because the publisher no longer supports the printing press. This would be unacceptable in any other medium. It must be unacceptable here, too. Games are art. They are also technology. But above all, when sold to consumers as complete packages, they must be protected by the same consumer rights as any other good. If a publisher or platform chooses to sell a game, they should have a legal obligation to ensure its long-term usability, at least in the form it was advertised. If a game is shut down, consumers should have the right to a refund, or ideally, to a version of the game that can be preserved, accessed, and played offline or independently. The European Union has long been a leader in digital rights, privacy, and consumer protection. The Digital Fairness Act is a step toward safeguarding these rights in the 21st century. But to be truly fair, it must confront the growing trend of killing digital gamesproducts that, once sold, should belong to the customer, not be taken back or shut down by corporate discretion. This is not a plea for punishment, but for preservation. For fairness. For the idea that when you buy something, it should be yours. Let us not allow our digital culture to fade into silence, server shutdowns, and legal fine print. Let us build a digital economy where art, ownership, and fairness can coexist. We thank you for your time, and we ask: please, stop the killing of games. Respectfully, Aleksandar Stamenkovic
